{"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-11","facts":["A confidence interval estimates a population parameter using sample data.","A 95% confidence interval procedure captures the true parameter in repeated samples about 95% of the time.","Wider confidence levels produce wider intervals when sample size stays fixed.","Increasing sample size usually narrows a confidence interval by reducing standard error.","A confidence interval balances point estimate and margin of error."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"confidence interval","df":"An interval of plausible values for a population parameter, computed from sample data using a chosen method and confidence level.","eg":"A 95% confidence interval for a mean might be 18.4 to 22.1.","mi":"It is a fixed range that has a 95% chance of containing the parameter for this one sample.","cx":"The parameter is fixed; the method has long-run success rates across repeated sampling."},{"tr":"margin of error","df":"The amount added to and subtracted from a point estimate to form the interval.","eg":"A sample mean of 50 with margin of error 3 gives an interval from 47 to 53.","mi":"It is the same as the standard deviation.","cx":"It is built from the standard error and a critical value, not from raw spread alone."},{"tr":"confidence level","df":"The long-run proportion of intervals from the same method that contain the true parameter.","eg":"A 90% confidence method is expected to capture the true mean in 90% of repeated samples.","mi":"It means 90% of the data values fall inside the interval.","cx":"It describes the method’s reliability, not the proportion of observations in the interval."}],"scenario":{"su":"A hospital measures the average waiting time for 40 randomly selected patients and finds a sample mean of 32 minutes. The staff want to estimate the true average waiting time for all patients this month.","ma":"The sample size, sample mean, and a confidence interval procedure for a mean."},"discussionPrompt":{"pr":"Should a policy decision rely more on a narrow confidence interval with a small sample or a wider interval with a larger sample?","tn":"Reasonable positions differ because precision, cost, and the risk of misleading estimates can point in different directions.","po":["Prefer the larger sample because it gives a more trustworthy interval.","Prefer the narrower interval if the estimate is needed immediately.","Use the interval only if the study design and sampling method are sound."]},"assignmentCore":{"td":"Analyze a short dataset of sample means and compute a confidence interval for a mean or proportion. Then explain in plain language what the interval does and does not say about the parameter.","pa":["Use one dataset with a single quantitative variable.","Report the interval, the confidence level, and one-sentence interpretation.","State one common misconception and correct it.","Keep the response concise and mathematically clear."]},"mc":[{"q":"What does a 95% confidence interval mean in introductory statistics?","op":["The method produces intervals that capture the true parameter about 95% of the time in repeated sampling.","There is a 95% probability that the true parameter lies in this one interval.","95% of the sample data values must fall inside the interval.","The interval guarantees the true parameter is inside it for this sample."],"ai":0,"ex":"A confidence level describes the long-run performance of the method, not the probability for one fixed interval.","fi":[1]},{"q":"A sample mean is 120 and the margin of error is 8. What is the confidence interval?","op":["112 to 128","118 to 128","104 to 136","120 to 128"],"ai":0,"ex":"A confidence interval is the point estimate plus and minus the margin of error: 120 ± 8 = 112 to 128.","fi":[4]},{"q":"Two intervals estimate the same mean with the same confidence level. Which interval is usually wider?","op":["The interval from the smaller sample","The interval from the larger sample","Both intervals are always the same width","The interval with the smaller standard deviation is always wider"],"ai":0,"ex":"Smaller samples usually have larger standard errors, which makes confidence intervals wider.","fi":[1,3]},{"q":"A student claims that a 99% confidence interval is better because it is always more accurate than a 95% interval. What is the best evaluation?","op":["The claim is incomplete because higher confidence usually makes the interval wider.","The claim is correct because higher confidence always gives a smaller margin of error.","The claim is correct because confidence level measures sample size.","The claim is incorrect because confidence intervals cannot be compared."],"ai":0,"ex":"Higher confidence requires a larger critical value, which increases the margin of error and widens the interval.","fi":[3,4]}],"studyGuide":{"sm":"Connect confidence interval, margin of error, and confidence level by explaining these claims: A confidence interval estimates a population parameter using sample data. A 95% confidence interval procedure captures the true parameter in repeated samples about 95% of the time.","rs":"Retrieve each definition and correction, then apply the three terms to the scenario without notes."},"workedExample":{"wp":"A sample of 64 students has a mean test score of 78 and a known standard deviation of 16. Find an approximate 95% confidence interval for the population mean using 2 as the critical value.","ws":["Compute the standard error: 16 / sqrt(64) = 16 / 8 = 2.","Compute the margin of error: 2 × 2 = 4.","Form the interval: 78 ± 4."],"wr":"(74, 82)"}}]}

{"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-11","facts":["Viruses require host cells to replicate their genomes and make viral proteins.","The viral life cycle includes attachment, entry, uncoating, replication, assembly, and release.","Attachment depends on viral surface proteins binding specific host-cell receptors.","Some viruses replicate in the nucleus, while others replicate in the cytoplasm.","Enveloped viruses often exit by budding, which acquires a lipid envelope."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"attachment","df":"The stage in which a virus binds to a susceptible host cell through specific interactions between viral proteins and host-cell receptors.","eg":"Influenza virus binding to receptors on respiratory epithelial cells.","mi":"Any virus can attach to any cell because all cells have the same surface molecules.","cx":"Attachment is receptor-specific, so host range depends on matching viral proteins and cell receptors."},{"tr":"uncoating","df":"The process in which the viral capsid is removed or disassembled so the viral genome becomes available for replication and gene expression.","eg":"A virus releasing its RNA after entering a host cell.","mi":"Uncoating is the same as viral release from the cell.","cx":"Uncoating happens after entry and before replication, whereas release happens at the end of the cycle."},{"tr":"assembly","df":"The stage in which newly made viral genomes and proteins are put together to form complete virions.","eg":"Packaging viral RNA into capsids near the end of replication.","mi":"Assembly means the virus is already fully infectious before components are packaged.","cx":"Assembly is the construction step that creates new virions from separately synthesized parts."}],"scenario":{"su":"A respiratory virus enters a host cell after binding a surface receptor. Inside the cell, its genome is released, copied, and used to make viral proteins. New particles are assembled, and the cell later releases them by a membrane-associated process.","ma":"A stepwise description of the viral life cycle and the host-cell membrane involvement in release."},"discussionPrompt":{"pr":"Should antiviral strategies focus more on blocking entry or blocking viral replication once entry has occurred?","tn":"Reasonable positions differ because entry-blocking can prevent infection early, while replication-blocking can still reduce spread after infection begins.","po":["Blocking entry is best because it prevents the virus from establishing infection.","Blocking replication is best because it can limit viral amplification inside infected cells.","Both targets matter because different viruses and stages of infection respond differently."]},"assignmentCore":{"td":"Analyze a viral life-cycle case by identifying which stage is disrupted by a given intervention and predicting the effect on viral spread. Explain how the intervention changes attachment, entry, replication, assembly, or release.","pa":["Use one case scenario only.","Write 150-250 words.","Name at least two life-cycle stages.","Do not use outside examples not tied to the life cycle."]},"mc":[{"q":"Which stage of the viral life cycle is the point at which a virus first binds to a host cell receptor?","op":["Attachment","Uncoating","Assembly","Release"],"ai":0,"ex":"Attachment is the initial binding of viral surface proteins to specific host-cell receptors.","fi":[2]},{"q":"A virus enters a cell and then releases its genome so replication can begin. Which stage is this?","op":["Attachment","Uncoating","Budding","Lysis"],"ai":1,"ex":"Uncoating is the release of the viral genome from the capsid after entry.","fi":[1]},{"q":"A clinician is comparing two viruses. One exits by taking a membrane from the host cell, while the other exits by breaking the cell open. What is the best diagnosis?","op":["The first is enveloped; the second is nonenveloped","The first is nonenveloped; the second is enveloped","Both must be nonenveloped viruses","Both must leave by uncoating"],"ai":0,"ex":"Budding acquires a lipid envelope, whereas lysis is a common release route for nonenveloped viruses.","fi":[4]},{"q":"Which claim about viral replication is most accurate?","op":["Viruses can replicate independently if conditions are favorable","Viruses always replicate in the nucleus","Viruses depend on host cells to make genomes and proteins","Viruses do not need to enter cells to reproduce"],"ai":2,"ex":"Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites and depend on host cells for replication and protein synthesis.","fi":[0]}],"studyGuide":{"sm":"Connect attachment, uncoating, and assembly by explaining these claims: Viruses require host cells to replicate their genomes and make viral proteins. The viral life cycle includes attachment, entry, uncoating, replication, assembly, and release.","rs":"Retrieve each definition and correction, then apply the three terms to the scenario without notes."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-1","facts":["Homeostasis maintains a stable internal environment despite external changes.","Negative feedback reduces a deviation from a set point.","A receptor detects a change in the internal condition.","A control center compares input to the set point and signals a response.","An effector carries out the corrective action."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"homeostasis","df":"The body's dynamic process of keeping internal conditions within narrow ranges compatible with normal cell function.","eg":"Regulating body temperature around 37°C during exercise.","mi":"Homeostasis means the body stays exactly the same all the time.","cx":"Internal conditions fluctuate, but they are actively regulated around acceptable ranges."},{"tr":"negative feedback","df":"A control mechanism in which a change triggers responses that oppose the original change.","eg":"Sweating when body temperature rises.","mi":"Negative feedback makes the original change stronger.","cx":"The response counteracts the deviation rather than amplifying it."},{"tr":"receptor","df":"A sensor that detects a change in a monitored variable and sends information to the control center.","eg":"Thermoreceptors detecting an increase in body temperature.","mi":"A receptor performs the corrective action itself.","cx":"A receptor detects change; it does not directly restore balance."}],"scenario":{"su":"A patient is walking briskly on a warm day and begins to feel hot. Skin temperature rises, and sweat appears on the forehead. The body must reduce the temperature change and return toward its normal range.","ma":"A brief patient scenario describing rising body temperature, sweating, and return toward normal temperature."},"discussionPrompt":{"pr":"In clinical care, when should nurses view a physiologic change as a normal homeostatic response versus an early sign of dysfunction?","tn":"Reasonable disagreement arises because the same response can be adaptive in one context and concerning in another.","po":["Some changes are normal compensations that preserve balance.","Some changes signal that compensatory mechanisms are failing.","Interpretation depends on the variable involved and the patient context."]},"assignmentCore":{"td":"Analyze a patient situation involving one homeostatic variable, identify the receptor, control center, effector, and corrective response, and explain how negative feedback restores balance.","pa":["Use one variable only.","Write 150-250 words.","Include the sequence stimulus, receptor, control center, effector, response.","Use nursing vocabulary precisely."]},"mc":[{"q":"Which statement best describes negative feedback in homeostasis?","op":["It reverses a deviation from a set point.","It strengthens a deviation from a set point.","It prevents any change in internal conditions.","It acts only during illness."],"ai":0,"ex":"Negative feedback counteracts the original change and helps return the variable toward its set point.","fi":[1,3]},{"q":"A patient’s body temperature rises during exercise. Which response best illustrates negative feedback?","op":["Sweat glands activate to help lower body temperature.","Body temperature continues to rise without regulation.","The brain stops monitoring temperature changes.","Muscles generate more heat to maintain the rise."],"ai":0,"ex":"Sweating promotes heat loss, opposing the temperature increase.","fi":[1,3]},{"q":"A student says the receptor is the structure that performs the corrective action. What is the best correction?","op":["The receptor detects change, while the effector performs the response.","The receptor compares the input to the set point.","The receptor is the same as the control center.","The receptor produces the final correction alone."],"ai":0,"ex":"In a feedback loop, detection and correction are separate functions.","fi":[2,4]},{"q":"Which claim about homeostasis is most accurate?","op":["It maintains internal stability through continuous adjustment.","It keeps internal conditions exactly constant at all times.","It works only when a disease is present.","It depends on a single organ acting alone."],"ai":0,"ex":"Homeostasis is dynamic regulation, not fixed immobility.","fi":[0]}],"studyGuide":{"sm":"Connect homeostasis, negative feedback, and receptor by explaining these claims: Homeostasis maintains a stable internal environment despite external changes. Negative feedback reduces a deviation from a set point.","rs":"Retrieve each definition and correction, then apply the three terms to the scenario without notes."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-13","facts":["Infectious disease transmission depends on a chain of linked events.","The chain of infection includes an infectious agent, reservoir, portal of exit, mode of transmission, portal of entry, and susceptible host.","Breaking any link in the chain can prevent spread of infection.","Direct contact transmits pathogens through person-to-person physical contact.","Indirect contact can occur through contaminated surfaces, equipment, or hands."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"chain of infection","df":"A model describing the sequence of conditions that allows a pathogen to spread from one host to another.","eg":"A respiratory virus leaves an infected person, spreads by droplets, enters another person's mucosa, and causes illness.","mi":"It is a list of steps that must all happen for every infection in exactly the same way.","cx":"The links are a useful model, but the specific route and order of spread can vary by organism and situation."},{"tr":"droplet transmission","df":"Spread of pathogens in relatively large respiratory particles that travel a short distance before settling.","eg":"Coughing or sneezing near another person without masking or separation.","mi":"Droplets behave like airborne particles and can travel long distances easily.","cx":"Droplets usually fall quickly, so close-range exposure is the main concern."},{"tr":"airborne transmission","df":"Spread of pathogens in very small particles that can remain suspended in air and be inhaled.","eg":"Infection risk in a poorly ventilated room after an infected person exhales infectious particles.","mi":"Airborne transmission only occurs during direct face-to-face contact.","cx":"Airborne particles may spread beyond immediate close contact and can persist in shared air."}],"scenario":{"su":"A nurse cares for a patient with a productive cough in a crowded clinic waiting area. Another patient sits nearby, and both share the same space for several minutes before either is masked.","ma":"The care setting, proximity of patients, respiratory symptoms, shared indoor air, and masking status."},"discussionPrompt":{"pr":"Which link in the chain of infection is most important to interrupt first in a busy nursing unit, and why?","tn":"Reasonable answers differ because the most effective break point depends on the organism, the setting, and which controls are feasible.","po":["Focus on the portal of exit and source control to reduce spread at the origin.","Focus on the mode of transmission because interrupting spread protects many people at once.","Focus on the susceptible host by using vaccines, hygiene, and other protective measures."]},"assignmentCore":{"td":"Analyze a short infection-control case and identify every link in the chain of infection that appears in the scenario. Then recommend one intervention for each link you identify.","pa":["Use the chain of infection framework explicitly.","Keep recommendations specific to nursing practice.","Write in concise clinical language.","Base the analysis only on the scenario details provided."]},"mc":[{"q":"Which sequence correctly lists the links in the chain of infection?","op":["Infectious agent, reservoir, portal of exit, mode of transmission, portal of entry, susceptible host","Reservoir, infectious agent, portal of entry, portal of exit, susceptible host, mode of transmission","Infectious agent, portal of entry, reservoir, mode of transmission, portal of exit, susceptible host","Susceptible host, portal of entry, mode of transmission, reservoir, infectious agent, portal of exit"],"ai":0,"ex":"The standard chain begins with the infectious agent and proceeds through reservoir, exit, transmission, entry, and susceptible host.","fi":[1]},{"q":"A nurse cleans a shared blood pressure cuff after use on a patient with an infectious illness. Which mode of transmission is most directly reduced?","op":["Indirect contact transmission through contaminated equipment","Droplet transmission through short-range respiratory particles","Airborne transmission through suspended fine particles","Vector-borne transmission through an insect carrier"],"ai":0,"ex":"A contaminated cuff can act as a fomite, so cleaning it reduces indirect contact transmission.","fi":[4]},{"q":"Two patients sit close together during coughing in a waiting room. Which feature best supports droplet transmission rather than airborne transmission?","op":["Large particles settle quickly after short travel","Particles remain suspended for hours in still air","A vector carries the organism between hosts","Transmission requires contaminated food or water"],"ai":0,"ex":"Droplets are relatively large and usually travel only a short distance before settling.","fi":[1]},{"q":"A classmate claims that breaking only one link in the chain of infection is usually enough to stop all transmission. Which response is best?","op":["The claim is too absolute because different links may need to be interrupted together","The claim is correct because every pathogen uses the same single route","The claim is correct because host susceptibility is the only important link","The claim is too absolute because infection cannot be interrupted at any point"],"ai":0,"ex":"Multiple controls are often used together because transmission depends on several links, and one intervention may not fully prevent spread in every situation.","fi":[1,2]}],"studyGuide":{"sm":"Connect chain of infection, droplet transmission, and airborne transmission by explaining these claims: Infectious disease transmission depends on a chain of linked events. The chain of infection includes an infectious agent, reservoir, portal of exit, mode of transmission, portal of entry, and susc","rs":"Retrieve each definition and correction, then apply the three terms to the scenario without notes."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-2","facts":["Cells are the smallest living units of the human body.","Groups of similar cells form tissues.","Tissues combine to make organs.","Organs work together in organ systems.","Structure and function are linked at every level of organization."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"cell","df":"The basic structural and functional unit of life in the human body.","eg":"A red blood cell transports oxygen in the bloodstream.","mi":"A cell is just a tiny body part with no independent function.","cx":"Cells carry out specific life processes and are the foundation of higher levels of organization."},{"tr":"tissue","df":"A tissue is a group of similar cells and extracellular material performing a shared function.","eg":"Epithelial tissue lines body surfaces and cavities.","mi":"Any collection of nearby cells automatically forms a tissue.","cx":"Cells must be similar and organized for a common function to count as tissue."},{"tr":"organ","df":"An organ is a structure made of two or more tissue types working together for a specific function.","eg":"The heart contains muscle, connective, nerve, and epithelial tissues.","mi":"An organ is just a larger version of a tissue.","cx":"Organs are composed of multiple tissues, not a single tissue type."}],"scenario":{"su":"A patient has skin that is healing after a minor cut. New cells are dividing at the wound edge, nearby connective tissue is repairing the damaged area, and blood vessels are delivering oxygen and nutrients to support recovery.","ma":"A short patient scenario describing wound healing, cell division, tissue repair, and blood supply"},"discussionPrompt":{"pr":"Why is it important for nurses to think about the body as organized from cells to organ systems when assessing a patient?","tn":"Reasonable positions differ because some problems appear local at the tissue level but actually involve whole-system coordination.","po":["Focus on the local tissue or organ where symptoms first appear.","Trace how multiple organ systems contribute to the patient’s problem.","Use both local and systemic perspectives because body functions are interconnected."]},"assignmentCore":{"td":"Analyze a brief clinical case and identify the cell, tissue, organ, and organ system levels involved in the patient’s problem. Explain how the levels are related in one coherent paragraph.","pa":["One paragraph, 150-200 words.","Use only the listed case details.","Name at least one example of each level: cell, tissue, organ, and organ system.","Use correct anatomical terminology."]},"mc":[{"q":"Which sequence correctly orders the levels of structural organization in the human body from simplest to most complex?","op":["Cell, tissue, organ, organ system","Tissue, cell, organ, organ system","Cell, organ, tissue, organ system","Organ, tissue, cell, organ system"],"ai":0,"ex":"Cells combine to form tissues, tissues combine to form organs, and organs work together in organ systems.","fi":[0,3]},{"q":"A nurse is explaining how a healing wound develops new protective covering. Which level is most directly described by a group of similar cells working together?","op":["Cell","Tissue","Organ","Organ system"],"ai":1,"ex":"A tissue consists of similar cells performing a shared function, such as repair or protection.","fi":[1,4]},{"q":"The heart contains muscle, connective, nerve, and epithelial tissues working together to pump blood. Which level of organization is the heart?","op":["Cell","Tissue","Organ","Organ system"],"ai":2,"ex":"An organ is made of two or more tissue types that cooperate for a specific function.","fi":[4]},{"q":"A student says, 'The respiratory system works independently because each organ has its own job.' Which response is most accurate?","op":["Correct, because organs never interact with other organs.","Incorrect, because organs within a system coordinate to maintain homeostasis.","Correct, because organ systems function only at rest.","Incorrect, because tissues are the highest level of organization."],"ai":1,"ex":"Organ systems are interconnected and coordinate their actions, especially to maintain homeostasis.","fi":[0,3]}],"studyGuide":{"sm":"Connect cell, tissue, and organ by explaining these claims: Cells are the smallest living units of the human body. {"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-9","facts":["Correlation measures the strength and direction of association between two variables.","A correlation does not identify whether one variable causes the other.","A third variable can create a spurious association between two variables.","Reverse causation can make the direction of influence opposite to what is assumed.","Random assignment helps support causal conclusions by balancing lurking variables."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"correlation","df":"A numerical summary of how two quantitative variables vary together, indicating direction and strength of a linear relationship.","eg":"Height and weight are often positively correlated in a sample of adults.","mi":"A correlation coefficient directly tells how much one variable causes the other to change.","cx":"A correlation describes association only; it does not establish a cause-and-effect relationship."},{"tr":"causation","df":"A relationship in which changes in one variable produce changes in another variable, all else being controlled.","eg":"Increasing fertilizer can cause plant growth to increase when other conditions are held constant.","mi":"Any two variables that move together must have a causal relationship.","cx":"Variables can be associated because of chance, confounding, or reverse direction rather than direct causation."},{"tr":"confounding variable","df":"A variable related to both the explanatory and response variables that can distort the apparent relationship between them.","eg":"Ice cream sales and drowning deaths may both rise because of hot weather.","mi":"A confounder is just a variable with a small effect that can be ignored.","cx":"A confounder can materially change the interpretation of an observed association and must be considered."}],"scenario":{"su":"A city reports that neighborhoods with more library visits also have higher average test scores. {"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-12","facts":["Antimicrobial resistance occurs when microorganisms survive exposure to drugs meant to kill or inhibit them.","Resistance can arise through mutation or acquisition of resistance genes.","Overuse and misuse of antimicrobials increase selection pressure for resistant strains.","Incomplete or incorrect dosing can allow partially resistant organisms to survive.","Resistance in one organism can spread through transmission in healthcare settings."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"antimicrobial resistance","df":"The ability of microorganisms to withstand the effects of an antimicrobial that would normally kill them or stop their growth.","eg":"A bacterial infection persists despite an antibiotic that previously worked against similar infections.","mi":"Resistance means the body has become used to the drug.","cx":"Resistance is a property of the microorganism, not the patient."},{"tr":"selection pressure","df":"An environmental force that favors survival of organisms with a trait that improves survival under those conditions.","eg":"Frequent antibiotic exposure makes resistant bacteria more likely to survive and multiply.","mi":"Antibiotics directly create resistance in every microorganism they contact.","cx":"Antibiotics select for preexisting resistant organisms; they do not make every organism resistant."},{"tr":"antimicrobial stewardship","df":"The coordinated use of antimicrobials to improve outcomes while limiting unnecessary exposure and resistance.","eg":"Choosing an antibiotic only when a bacterial infection is likely and narrowing therapy when possible.","mi":"Stewardship means avoiding all antimicrobials.","cx":"Stewardship supports appropriate use, not refusal of needed treatment."}],"scenario":{"su":"A nurse cares for two hospitalized patients.
